title: Quiz: Magic States and Quantum Computation
description: Test your understanding of the resource theory of magic and its role in quantum computing

questions.question: According to the Gottesman-Knill theorem, which circuits can be efficiently simulated classically?

options.text: Any circuit with only single-qubit gates
Circuits with Clifford gates, computational basis preparation and measurement
Any circuit with less than 50 qubits
Circuits containing T gates

questions.explanation: The Gottesman-Knill theorem states that circuits with only Clifford gates (H, S, CNOT), preparation in the computational basis, and measurements in the computational basis can be efficiently simulated classically, despite potentially creating large amounts of entanglement.

questions.question: What are the free states in the resource theory of magic?

options.text: Pure states only
Stabilizer states
Product states
States with positive Wigner function everywhere

questions.explanation: The free states are stabilizer states - states that are uniquely defined as the +1 eigenstate of an abelian subgroup of the Pauli group. These states can be efficiently described classically and manipulated by Clifford operations.

questions.question: The T-state |T⟩ = (|0⟩ + e^{iπ/4}|1⟩)/√2 is important because:

options.text: It is the most entangled single-qubit state
It enables universal quantum computation when combined with Clifford gates
It is a stabilizer state with special properties
It has zero magic

questions.explanation: The T-state is a magic (non-stabilizer) state that, when combined with Clifford operations via gate teleportation, enables implementation of the T-gate. Clifford + T is a universal gate set for quantum computation.

questions.question: What is magic state distillation?

options.text: Creating magic states from thermal states
Converting noisy magic states to fewer high-fidelity magic states using Clifford operations
Measuring magic states to extract classical information
Transforming stabilizer states into magic states

questions.explanation: Magic state distillation uses many copies of noisy (low-fidelity) magic states and Clifford operations to produce fewer copies of high-fidelity magic states. This is essential for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

questions.question: Clifford operations alone (without magic states) can create magic from stabilizer states.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Clifford operations are the free operations in the resource theory of magic - they preserve the stabilizer property. Magic cannot be created from stabilizer states using only Clifford operations; this is the essence of why magic is a resource.

questions.question: The stabilizer extent ξ(|ψ⟩) measures:

options.text: The number of qubits in the state
The minimum l₁-norm of coefficients when decomposing |ψ⟩ into stabilizer states
The entanglement entropy of the state
The fidelity with the nearest stabilizer state

questions.explanation: The stabilizer extent is the minimum sum of absolute values of coefficients needed to express a state as a superposition of stabilizer states. It determines the classical simulation cost: circuits with magic scale exponentially in the extent.

questions.question: For the 15-to-1 distillation protocol with input error rate ε, the output error rate is approximately:

options.text: ε²
35ε³

questions.explanation: The 15-to-1 protocol achieves cubic error suppression: if the input states have error rate ε, the output has error rate approximately 35ε³. This rapid error suppression (when ε is small) is why distillation is practical.

questions.question: Which statements about magic and quantum computation are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Stabilizer states can have non-zero entanglement
The T-gate is a Clifford gate
Classical simulation cost scales exponentially with the number of T-gates
Fault-tolerant T-gates are typically implemented via state injection

questions.explanation: (a) Correct: GHZ and Bell states are stabilizer states with maximal entanglement. (b) Incorrect: T is NOT a Clifford gate - it's outside the Clifford group. (c) Correct: Cost scales as ξ^(2t) where t is T-gate count. (d) Correct: T-gates use magic state injection and gate teleportation in fault-tolerant schemes.
